text:
This is the central theme of Tolkien’s story, “Leaf by Niggle”.

He wrote it during a rough stretch in the middle of the LOTR project.

It’s a story about work, vocation, and creativity. And how to view distractions, interruptions, and incomplete projects.

text: “There are no ordinary people. You have never talked to a mere mortal. Nations, cultures, arts, civilizations—these are mortal, and their life is to ours as the life of a gnat. But it is immortals whom we joke with, work with, marry, snub, and exploit—immortal horrors or everlasting splendors." - C.S. Lewis

text:
"What is The Compendium?", you may ask.

It is a vast repository of fantastic objects of myth and legend, gathered from worlds beyond our own and currently located under their school.

Each object is secured in one of the museum's wings - The Narnia Wing, The Ashtown Wing, etc. https://t.co/ooZV9ICoiY

text:
When I created this story for my kids a few years ago, my youngest was not in school, so she missed out.

And she has reminded me about this multiple times. So I am finally getting around to a sequel.

This week started their second serial story experience: The Compendium.

raw.full_text:
When I created this story for my kids a few years ago, my youngest was not in school, so she missed out.

And she has reminded me about this multiple times. So I am finally getting around to a sequel.

This week started their second serial story experience: The Compendium.

text:
But some of the objects from the Narnia wing have inexplicably vanished!

This is where the kids come in.

In this first week, they have received a formal letter from the director, briefing them on the situation and enlisting them as Artifact Retrieval Specialists. https://t.co/sVqabV2ayA

text:
Every week, I'll give them a riddle to help locate one of the missing objects on the school property.

Throw in some plot points around the mystery of who stole the objects and a few surprises from the mischievous characters.

And it should be a fun way to finish the semester.

text:
@Jeanvaljean689 1. Proximity. Purchased a rack/bench and put it 20ft from my desk (I work from home). Basketball hoop out in front of my house.

2. Involve family. Work out with my wife 3 days a week. Play basketball with my kids 3-4 days a week. That's ~75% of my weekly exercise.

text:
Social media is fascinating. Thousands of people evaluating everything (and each other) in real time.

But criticism (positive or negative) almost always reveals more about the critic than the work.

A few thoughts loosely based on Experiment in Criticism (CSL).

raw.full_text:
Social media is fascinating. Thousands of people evaluating everything (and each other) in real time.

But criticism (positive or negative) almost always reveals more about the critic than the work.

A few thoughts loosely based on Experiment in Criticism (CSL).

text:
Criticism without humility is worthless.

Lewis: “We are so busy doing things with the work that we give it too little chance to work on us... The first demand any work of any art makes upon us is surrender. Look. Listen. Receive. Get out of the way.”

Receive. Then evaluate.

raw.full_text:
Criticism without humility is worthless.

Lewis: “We are so busy doing things with the work that we give it too little chance to work on us... The first demand any work of any art makes upon us is surrender. Look. Listen. Receive. Get out of the way.”

Receive. Then evaluate.

text:
A shallow reader (viewer, listener, etc.) doesn’t just miss the point. They create a new one and then critique that.

You think they’re telling you something about the book, the movie, the idea. But they’re really just telling you something about themselves.

raw.full_text:
A shallow reader (viewer, listener, etc.) doesn’t just miss the point. They create a new one and then critique that.

You think they’re telling you something about the book, the movie, the idea. But they’re really just telling you something about themselves.

text:
Lewis said that if even one person could love for a lifetime something that you thought was bad, it should raise the suspicion that it can't be as bad as you thought.

So what damns something is not the existence of bad takes but the absence of good ones.

raw.full_text:
Lewis said that if even one person could love for a lifetime something that you thought was bad, it should raise the suspicion that it can't be as bad as you thought.

So what damns something is not the existence of bad takes but the absence of good ones.

text:
This is why good criticism is rare. You have to risk being “taken in.”

You can’t know if the work is bad unless you first try to read it as if it were very good. And only then discover it doesn’t hold up and that you were paying the artist an undeserved compliment.

raw.full_text:
This is why good criticism is rare. You have to risk being “taken in.”

You can’t know if the work is bad unless you first try to read it as if it were very good. And only then discover it doesn’t hold up and that you were paying the artist an undeserved compliment.

text:
Good critics assume that there may be something there that they just can’t see yet.

They are "easy to please but hard to satisfy".

raw.full_text:
Good critics assume that there may be something there that they just can’t see yet.

They are "easy to please but hard to satisfy".

text:
Lewis believed that negative criticism should require more work (i.e. "a negative proposition is harder to establish than a positive").

This is why short responses work if they're positive (e.g. “Love this!”) but often come off lazy and unserious when they're dismissive.

raw.full_text:
Lewis believed that negative criticism should require more work (i.e. "a negative proposition is harder to establish than a positive").

This is why short responses work if they're positive (e.g. “Love this!”) but often come off lazy and unserious when they're dismissive.

text:
Judge posture first. Then you can fairly judge taste.

Lewis: "How men read is a strong basis for judgments on what they read."

Most people reverse this: "This person likes x, so they must have bad taste."

But why people like things is more important than what they like.

raw.full_text:
Judge posture first. Then you can fairly judge taste.

Lewis: "How men read is a strong basis for judgments on what they read."

Most people reverse this: "This person likes x, so they must have bad taste."

But why people like things is more important than what they like.

text:
Modern criticism is mostly vague posturing (e.g. cringe, mid). It's verbicide.

To judge fairly, you need to describe the effect of the work. And whether it fits.

Word count here is like rope. Give bad critics enough of it and they'll hang themselves.

https://t.co/fC0OoV1f7F

text:
So with art, the question really isn't: “But is it good tho?”

It's more like:

What kind of attention does it invite?
What kind of people does it attract?
What kind of person do you have to be to really see it?

raw.full_text:

It's more like:

What kind of people does it attract?
What kind of person do you have to be to really see it?

text:
@visakanv One of the insights from CSL's book on criticism is that constructive criticism is more interesting. But it (should) require more work.

So the lack of time disproportionately filters out the interesting replies.

What's left tends to be shrill or flat.

https://t.co/J5g0rK0hkO
